why_correct: The Anthropic Messages API is fully stateless — no session object, no server-side history, nothing persists between calls. Conversational continuity is entirely the client's responsibility: you must reconstruct the full alternating human/assistant turn sequence on every request. Omitting assistant replies breaks the alternating turn structure and leaves Claude with only the user's half of the conversation, so any reference to prior assistant output has no grounding in the context window.

distractors.A: A missing system prompt affects persona and behavioral constraints, not memory of prior exchanges. Claude would still see turn 1's user message and its own reply if those were correctly appended — the role consistency framing is a red herring. A is the right diagnosis only if the failure were Claude breaking character mid-conversation, not amnesia about its own prior output.
distractors.C: Context window exhaustion is a real failure mode in long conversations, but it produces truncation of the oldest turns, not total amnesia from turn 1 to turn 2. The architectural tell here is that the failure appears immediately on turn 2 after a single exchange — there's no way a context limit was hit that fast on a normal refund policy response.
distractors.D: Streaming is a delivery mechanism for token throughput and time-to-first-token latency — it has no bearing on session state. The connection closes after the response stream completes. Switching to streaming changes nothing about the stateless request model.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for any multi-turn memory failure: the API is stateless by definition, so always audit the messages array being sent on each call. If the assistant turns are absent, Claude is effectively a amnesiac — it only knows what you explicitly pass in the payload.
cross_domain_note: This is the foundational pattern underlying agentic loop design — the orchestration layer owns the conversation buffer, and every tool-use or multi-step workflow depends on correctly accumulating both human and assistant turns across the full execution trace.

why_correct: The Messages API is stateless — the full conversational context must be reconstructed client-side and sent on every request. When you drop add_assistant_message(), the messages list contains only alternating user turns with no assistant turns, so Claude receives a malformed conversation structure where its own prior outputs are completely absent from the context window. Starting on turn 3, Claude is effectively responding to a new conversation each time — it has no record of what it committed to, what it clarified, or what it already answered. The failure mode is not subtle: any response that logically depends on a prior assistant turn will be incoherent or contradictory.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is assuming Claude has some out-of-band memory or inference channel for its own prior outputs. It doesn't. There is no mechanism by which user messages implicitly carry assistant context — the model sees only what's in the messages array. This answer conflates stateful session behavior with stateless API calls.
distractors.C: This is the dangerous near-miss. It correctly identifies that degradation won't manifest in turns 1-2 (because there's no prior assistant output to reference yet), but it invents a compensating mechanism that doesn't exist. System prompts carry persona and instructions, not conversation history. In production, this misdiagnosis leads teams to ship a broken chatbot because test coverage only hit shallow turn depths.
distractors.D: Context loss is structural, not conditional on user behavior. Whether the user explicitly references a prior reply is irrelevant — Claude's own reasoning continuity depends on seeing its own prior turns. A follow-up like 'can you expand on that?' and a seemingly unrelated question both break when the assistant's prior response is missing from the thread.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: the messages list is the entire universe Claude sees. If it's not in the array, it doesn't exist for that request. Every assistant turn you drop is a permanent, unrecoverable memory hole — there is no inference, no reconstruction, no compensation from system prompt or user context.
cross_domain_note: This directly connects to token budget and cost optimization decisions — the correct way to reduce token count across turns is prompt caching or context summarization, not dropping assistant turns, which trades cost savings for correctness.

why_correct: The root cause is stateful conversation context being stored in process-local memory behind a stateless load balancer — a classic horizontal scaling violation. Externalizing the messages list to a distributed cache (Redis being the canonical choice) keyed by session ID decouples conversation state from process affinity, so any of the four instances can reconstruct the full context window on each request. This satisfies the stated durability constraint — coherence across the session, but no requirement to survive restarts — without over-engineering to durable storage. It also eliminates the cross-contamination issue, which was caused by the request-scoped list being re-initialized per handler invocation rather than per session.

distractors.A: Sticky sessions fix the routing problem but preserve the underlying anti-pattern: state still lives in process memory. The failure mode is that any instance restart, autoscale event, or deployment drops that user's entire conversation context. In production under load, you will lose sessions constantly. Sticky sessions are the right answer only if the state store genuinely cannot be externalized — a constraint that doesn't exist here.
distractors.C: Cookie-based state serialization shifts the trust boundary in a dangerous direction — the client now holds the authoritative messages list, which is a prompt injection surface. A malicious user can tamper with conversation history to manipulate the assistant's context window, even with signing and encryption adding friction. It also bloats request/response payloads proportionally to conversation length, eventually hitting cookie size limits mid-session.
distractors.D: The stem explicitly states conversations do not need to survive process restarts — a relational DB is the right answer when you need durability and auditability, but that's not the requirement here. Reaching for a write-ahead-log-backed database when a TTL-keyed cache satisfies the SLA is over-engineering that adds unnecessary write latency to every conversational turn.

mental_model: When horizontal scaling breaks stateful behavior, always match the persistence tier to the stated durability requirement — not the maximum possible durability. Session coherence without restart survival maps directly to a distributed cache with TTL, not sticky routing (which re-couples you to process affinity) and not a database (which solves a durability problem you don't have).
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to token budget management in long sessions: externalizing the messages list to a cache also gives you the right architectural hook to implement context truncation or summarization before reconstructing the payload sent to the model, keeping you inside the context window as sessions grow.

why_correct: The root cause is process-local state in a horizontally scaled deployment — a module-level variable is scoped to the OS process, not the cluster. With four workers behind a round-robin load balancer, any given session hits a different worker ~75% of the time, each of which holds an empty messages list for that user. Externalizing per-session state to a distributed cache (Redis being the canonical choice) keyed by session ID decouples state lifetime from worker lifetime, so any worker can hydrate the full conversation context on any request.

distractors.A: A is the right diagnosis for a *different* bug — the shared-mutable-list concurrency problem where two users in the same process pollute each other's history. That's a real anti-pattern worth fixing, but it produces garbled context, not fresh-start context loss. The 15% figure is the tell: if intra-process corruption were the cause, you'd expect near-100% of users on a busy worker to be affected, not a consistent minority. A is right the moment you're running a single-process server; wrong here because the stem specifies four workers.
distractors.C: Sticky sessions look like a fix because they pin a user to one worker, preserving the in-process list. The failure mode is operational: sticky sessions collapse your horizontal scale benefit, create uneven load distribution when session counts skew, and make worker restarts (deploys, crashes, scaling events) cause full context loss for every pinned user simultaneously. It trades a stateless architecture for a stateful one at the infra layer without solving the underlying state externalization problem. This is the 'duct tape over the architecture' anti-pattern.
distractors.D: An asyncio.Lock() on append operations addresses a race condition that doesn't match the observed symptom. Append races within one worker would corrupt ordering or occasionally drop a turn, but wouldn't produce the clean 'fresh conversation' response the stem describes. Also, if the handler is synchronous (typical FastAPI with a thread pool), asyncio primitives don't apply to the concurrency model in use.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: any time you see 'module-level mutable state' plus 'multiple processes' plus 'load balancer,' the answer is always externalize to distributed storage — no infra trick (sticky sessions, locking) substitutes for correct state architecture. If state must outlive a single request and survive any worker, it belongs outside the process.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to prompt construction in agentic systems: the same externalized-messages-list pattern is what enables multi-turn tool-use loops to survive across async worker handoffs in an orchestration layer.

why_correct: The Anthropic client is a stateless HTTP transport layer — it holds no conversation context, no session affinity, no per-turn memory. All conversational state is explicitly passed in the messages array on every API call, which means concurrent invocations through a shared client instance cannot corrupt each other. Moving instantiation to the worker's initialization lifecycle gives you persistent connection pooling without any state isolation risk, directly eliminating the 38% connection-establishment overhead and the burst exhaustion problem simultaneously.

distractors.B: The 'session affinity metadata' claim is the misconception — the Anthropic API is stateless by design, no such routing metadata exists in the client object. This distractor is tempting because it sounds like a mature distributed-systems concern (think sticky sessions in load balancers), but that mental model doesn't transfer here. There's nothing in the client to partition.
distractors.C: An object pool capped at concurrency limit is architecturally sound in principle, but it misdiagnoses the root cause as 'too many simultaneous connections' when the actual root cause is 'repeated connection establishment per invocation.' At 60 RPS, even a perfectly sized object pool still re-establishes connections if clients aren't reused. This is the near-miss: it addresses pool exhaustion during bursts but leaves per-invocation connection overhead untouched.
distractors.D: Funneling 60 concurrent workers through a single orchestrator-level client introduces a serialization bottleneck that destroys your concurrency model. The orchestrator's request queue doesn't provide 'sufficient serialization' — it provides a chokepoint. The failure mode is throughput collapse under load, the exact opposite of the goal.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: client lifecycle scope should match the unit of concurrency, not the unit of work. If the client is stateless, instantiate once per worker (the concurrency unit) and pass all per-call state explicitly. Conflating 'shared client' with 'shared state' is the distractor trap — it only applies when the client actually holds mutable state.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the prompt construction domain: just as the client holds no conversation history, the assistant turn holds no memory across calls unless you explicitly reconstruct the messages array. Both are instances of the same design principle — explicit state passing over implicit session state.

why_correct: The junior engineer commits a category error: conflating stateful agent isolation (which the messages list correctly handles) with stateless infrastructure objects (which have no per-run state to isolate). The Anthropic client carries connection pool state that is expensive to construct and tear down — not agent-specific conversational state. Per-run instantiation gives you zero isolation benefit because there is nothing agent-specific to isolate, while costing you a new socket allocation on every run. Under 50 concurrent agents each making multi-step calls, you blow through the process file descriptor limit because pools are being created and destroyed faster than the OS reclaims them.

distractors.A: This inverts the correct architecture — per-run messages lists are exactly right for agent state isolation. The messages list is the isolation primitive; it carries the per-run context window. Moving it into a shared orchestrator would be the actual isolation failure, creating cross-run context bleed. A is wrong on the diagnosis and wrong on the fix.
distractors.C: The near-miss here is that 'once per session' reduces instantiation frequency, which might defer exhaustion under moderate load, but it doesn't fix the architectural error. The correct answer isn't 'instantiate less often' — it's 'recognize that the client is stateless across agent runs and share it at module scope.' C still accepts the junior engineer's flawed premise that the client needs run-scoped or session-scoped ownership.
distractors.D: The Anthropic client is designed to be thread-safe and carries no mutable per-agent-run state between API calls — the messages list is external to the client by design. Telling ops to raise the ulimit is a patch on a broken pattern; you'd need to raise it proportionally with every concurrency increase forever.

mental_model: Separate 'what needs isolation' from 'what needs sharing': stateful objects that carry per-run context (messages list, run ID, tool state) get instantiated per run; stateless infrastructure objects with expensive initialization (HTTP clients, connection pools, embedding model handles) get instantiated once at module or service scope and shared safely across concurrent runs. When you see file descriptor exhaustion under concurrency, the first question is always 'which expensive object is being constructed in the hot path?'
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to prompt caching strategy: the client-level connection pool is analogous to a cache that needs warm state to be useful — destroying and rebuilding it per-call is the same anti-pattern as disabling prompt caching on a high-frequency agentic loop, paying reconstruction cost on every turn with no reuse benefit.
